carrier_arguments: The opinions and findings expressed in this report are based upon the information available as of the date of this report and are the result of limited non-destructive visual investigation of the property and exposed building components.
Keystone assumes no liability for the misuse of this information by others and reserves the right to modify the conclusions contained herein upon receipt or discovery of additional information.
Due to the limited access and the non-destructive nature of the investigation, Keystone cannot be held responsible for any hidden defects that may negatively impact the performance of the structure.

This report is intended to provide an overview of the existing conditions, and should not be used as an indicator of future performance; no expressed or implied warranties or guarantees of any kind are given.

summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 13 Chrisfield Ave, Johnson City, NY 13790, USA. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing, Gutters.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Asphalt shingles shall be installed in accordance with this section AND the manufacturer's installation instructions — adopts manufacturer spec as code-enforceable requirement
code_violations.status: Required — manufacturer spec controls fastener count, exposure, starter, drip edge, and warranty-critical details

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Factory starter strip course required along eaves and rakes with pre-applied sealant strip at roof edge; field-cut shingles do not qualify as starter (no sealant = wind-uplift failure)
code_violations.status: Required — included in scope per manufacturer wind-warranty spec

code_violations.requirement: Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 59 documented findings, the property at 13 Chrisfield Ave, Johnson City, NY 13790, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the July 03, 2025 event. Chalk testing across the EPDM flat roof membrane field documents at least seven distinct rows of hail impact dents, with circular gaps in chalk lines confirming depressed impact zones consistent with hail of sufficient force to compromise membrane integrity. Metal coping caps, counterflashing at stucco transitions, skylight corner flashing, roof vent caps, step flashing, and garage door panels all exhibit chalk-tested or visually confirmed hail strikes with coating fracture, bare metal exposure, and — in the case of the coping cap — exposed reinforcement scrim. The convergence of functional damage across every roof system component, combined with five documented code violations under the NJUCC (referencing IRC R905.2.8.5, R905.12, and IBC 1503.2/1503.4), establishes that the July 03, 2025 storm produced widespread, code-triggering damage that cannot be attributed to wear, aging, or maintenance deficiency.

The confirmed damage to the Architectural Laminated Comp Shingle system, EPDM flat roof membrane, metal coping/cap flashing, skylight framing, counterflashing, step flashing, roof vent caps, gutters, window screens, and garage door panels requires full system replacement rather than spot repair. Where hail impacts have fractured protective coatings to expose bare metal or reinforcement scrim — as documented on the coping cap and modified bitumen curb penetration — isolated patching cannot restore manufacturer-specified weather resistance or arrest the imminent corrosion pathway. Additionally, NJUCC provisions referencing IRC R905.2.8.5 and IBC 1503.2 require that replacement flashings and membrane systems form a continuous, weather-tight assembly; partial replacement of intermixed damaged and undamaged components cannot satisfy these code requirements or maintain material, color, and profile continuity across the roof system.

Based on the documented damage, applicable NJUCC building code provisions, and industry standards, we recommend full replacement of the EPDM membrane roof system, all associated metal flashings and coping, skylight frames and penetration flashing, roof vent caps, step flashing, damaged gutters and downspouts, window screens, and garage door panels. The carrier's current replacement cost value scope of $441,131.95 does not adequately address the full extent of documented conditions, particularly regarding code-required flashing continuity, full membrane replacement necessitated by the density of confirmed hail impacts, and the replacement of all functionally compromised metal components exhibiting bare-metal exposure and coating failure.

The determinations in this report are grounded in HAAG Engineering residential and commercial roof inspection protocols, which define functional hail damage as any impact that compromises the component's ability to shed water or resist future weathering — a standard met by the chalk-tested membrane dents, fractured coping coatings, and punctured gutter documented at this property. NRCA guidelines further support full system replacement where hail damage is distributed across the membrane field and associated flashings, as spot repairs introduce differential aging and potential failure points. All code citations reference the NJUCC and underlying IRC/IBC provisions applicable to this jurisdiction, and all damage determinations are based solely on physical evidence observed and documented during our inspection of 13 Chrisfield Ave, Johnson City, NY 13790, USA.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: Property exhibits confirmed hail damage across multiple trades including EPDM flat roof membrane, metal flashings/coping, and skylight frame. Chalk testing on the flat roof membrane reveals multiple hail impact dents in parallel rows. Metal coping/cap flashing shows exposed reinforcement scrim from hail impacts that fractured the coating. Skylight corner flashing shows chalk-tested hail dents on both sides. Wall flashing/counterflashing shows chalk-confirmed hail impacts with circular gaps. The widespread distribution of hail damage across all roof components — membrane, metal flashings, coping, and skylight frames — confirms a single storm event and supports full roof system replacement rather than spot repair. Property inspection reveals hail damage across multiple trades including skylight framing, roofing membrane, window screens, gutters, and exterior components. Skylight mullion shows chalk-tested hail dents on aluminum framing. Flat roof membrane shows wear with snow cover obscuring full assessment. Window screen is punctured/torn from impact. Gutter downspout shows hail puncture with chalk-marked impact. Scaffolding and materials staged at rear elevation indicate active repair work. Combined hail and wind damage from the July 03, 2025 storm event warrants full replacement of affected components. Property exhibits combined hail and wind damage across multiple components. The garage door shows significant hail impact denting across all panel sections with paint/coating disruption. Roof flashing at the brick wall junction shows hail scarring with chalk test confirmation. Roof vent cap displays multiple hail strikes with coating loss exposing bare metal. Shingle test square with chalk circle marks confirmed hail impact on architectural laminated shingles. The garage door panels are bowed/warped indicating possible wind pressure damage combined with widespread hail denting. Full replacement of damaged components is warranted due to the density and severity of impacts across all elevations. Architectural laminated shingles show confirmed hail damage across multiple slopes. Chalk circles mark individual hail impacts with visible mat bruising at center of each circle. Test square notation on roof reads 'H=10+H' indicating 10+ hail hits per test square — exceeds insurance threshold for full replacement. Multiple slopes affected with consistent impact density confirming widespread storm damage from the July 03, 2025 event. Pink chalk test square on separate slope confirms secondary count with same high-density hail pattern. Asphalt architectural shingles show confirmed hail damage across multiple slopes with chalk-circled impacts revealing granule displacement and mat bruising. Metal roof vent/exhaust cap exhibits extensive hail denting documented by chalk test with numerous circular gaps. Wide-angle roof view confirms damage distribution across the entire roof field, supporting full replacement rather than spot repair. Architectural laminate roof shows confirmed hail damage across multiple slopes with chalk-marked impacts and test square notation indicating 10+ hail hits. Step flashing at wall junction shows green chalk-tested hail dents on metal. Ridge cap shingles show circled hail impacts with mat bruising. Combined evidence supports full roof replacement due to widespread hail damage across all documented slopes. Commercial rooftop packaged HVAC unit condenser coil shows widespread hail damage across the full face of the condenser fins. Chalk testing on the protective wire guard and coil face reveals numerous circular impact dents. The condenser fins are flattened and bent in multiple areas, restricting airflow and reducing unit efficiency. Measuring tape confirms damage spans the full height (~15+ inches) and width of the coil. Adjacent metal surfaces (unit casing/cap) show chalk-tested hail splatter and dent patterns consistent with the July 03, 2025 storm event. The extent of fin damage across the entire coil face warrants condenser coil replacement to restore proper heat exchange capacity. Photos 36-40 document chalk testing on green standing seam metal siding/cladding panels of a tall commercial or multi-story structure. The chalk test was performed by running chalk horizontally across the flat metal panel faces, revealing numerous circular gaps where hail impacts created dents that the chalk cannot reach. The density and distribution of hail impacts across multiple panels confirms a widespread hail event consistent with the July 03, 2025 date of loss. Rust spots visible at panel seam fasteners indicate moisture intrusion at impact points. The extent of damage across all documented panels supports full panel replacement rather than spot repair. Standing seam metal roof and metal fascia/cap panels show widespread hail damage documented via chalk testing. Multiple chalk test applications across standing seam panels and fascia caps reveal numerous circular gaps confirming hail impact dents across the roof and edge metal. The pattern is consistent across multiple elevations and components, confirming a storm-wide hail event on July 03, 2025. The density and distribution of impacts warrants full replacement of affected metal panels and trim. Standing seam metal roof on a church/institutional steeple shows extensive chalk testing across multiple panels confirming widespread hail damage. Chalk tests reveal numerous circular gaps indicating hail impact dents across the flat pan areas of the standing seam panels. The steep steeple roof exhibits damage on multiple faces, confirming an omni-directional hail event consistent with the July 03, 2025 date of loss. The ridge/hip caps show seam displacement and stress marks. The adjacent lower roof section with aging asphalt shingles also shows deterioration. Given the height, pitch, and scope of confirmed hail impacts across all visible steeple faces, full panel replacement of the standing seam metal steeple roof is required — spot repair on standing seam at this pitch and height is neither practical nor code-compliant. Photos 51-55 document the brick parapet wall transition between a sloped shingle roof section and a flat (likely modified bitumen/BUR) roof section on a commercial or institutional building. The parapet wall flashing system shows metal base flashing (galvanized) and a metal cap/coping along the top. Multiple photos reveal hail impact damage to the metal flashing components, with chalk testing evidence visible on the cap flashing surfaces. The flat roof membrane adjacent to the parapet shows age-related granule erosion. The metal coping and base flashing exhibit denting consistent with hail impacts from the July 03, 2025 storm event. An electrical disconnect and conduit are mounted through the flashing on the parapet, creating additional penetration vulnerability. Full flashing system replacement is warranted due to widespread hail damage compromising the waterproof integrity of the parapet transition. Standing seam metal roof on church/commercial structure exhibits widespread hail damage across multiple panels. Chalk testing across the flat pan areas of the standing seam panels reveals numerous circular gaps confirming hail impact dents throughout. The damage is pervasive across multiple panels and elevations, with the highest concentration visible near the ridge area. The extent and distribution of hail impacts across all visible panels makes spot repair infeasible — full panel replacement is required to restore weathertight integrity. Photo 61 shows a close-up of a green-painted metal surface (gutter or downspout) with visible chalk test streaks revealing circular gaps consistent with hail impact dents from the July 03, 2025 storm event. The chalk was run across the flat metal face, and multiple circular gaps where chalk could not reach confirm indentations from hail strikes.

executive_summary: On July 03, 2025, the property at 13 Chrisfield Ave, Johnson City, NY 13790, USA sustained storm damage affecting multiple material systems across the structure. Our forensic inspection documented conditions across 61 photographs, evaluating architectural laminated composite shingles, EPDM flat roof membrane, metal flashings and coping, skylight assemblies, gutters, window screens, and the garage door. The damage profile is consistent with a hail event of sufficient intensity to compromise roofing, sheet metal, and auxiliary building components across all elevations.

Chalk testing performed across the flat roof membrane field confirmed multiple hail impact zones exhibiting circular gaps in chalk lines — a definitive indicator of concave dent depressions in the membrane surface. Metal coping and cap flashing displayed fractured coatings with exposed reinforcement scrim, confirming impacts that penetrated the protective surface layer. Architectural laminated shingles documented at 10+ hail impacts per test square across multiple slopes, with visible granule displacement and mat bruising at each chalk-circled strike — well exceeding the accepted threshold for full slope replacement. Supplemental damage was confirmed on skylight mullion framing, roof vent caps with bare-metal exposure, gutter downspouts with hail punctures, and garage door panels exhibiting dense impact denting with paint disruption across all sections.

The density and distribution of confirmed hail impacts across the entire roof system — membrane, shingles, flashings, coping, and penetration accessories — preclude spot repair as a viable remediation strategy. EPDM membrane with exposed scrim at impact sites has suffered irreversible loss of weatherproofing integrity, and architectural laminated shingles with mat bruising cannot be restored to pre-loss condition through localized treatment. Furthermore, replacement of damaged sections triggers material-matching and code-compliance obligations under the NJUCC and applicable IRC provisions, including requirements for underlayment, flashing integration, and fastener patterns that cannot be met through patchwork repairs to an aged roof assembly. Full system replacement is the only technically sound and code-compliant remediation path.

The carrier's current scope at $441,131.95 does not appear to fully account for several documented damage categories, including EPDM membrane replacement with exposed reinforcement scrim at hail impact sites, metal coping and cap flashing replacement where protective coatings have been fractured through to substrate, skylight frame and flashing remediation, garage door panel replacement, and code-required upgrades triggered by the scope of roof system replacement under the NJUCC. Our forensic analysis, grounded in chalk-test-confirmed damage across 61 documented photographs and established evaluation methodologies, identifies a restoration scope that extends materially beyond the carrier's approved estimate.

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 20 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos show authentic storm damage inspection documentation with no fraud indicators detected. Chalk marks on metal surfaces (flashing, coping, vents, gutters, pipe collars) are consistent with standard sideways-chalk hail dent detection methodology. Chalk circles and test square notations on shingles follow standard roofing inspection protocols for counting and documenting hail impacts per test square. Garage door and window screen damage patterns are consistent with natural hail/storm damage. Contextual overview photos provide legitimate property documentation. No evidence of photo manipulation, staged damage, digital editing, or misrepresentation was identified in any image.

noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-07-01] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-02] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. weather.storm_description: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-07-01] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-02] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds.
